title: memory_libmap creates initialized registers but some architectures don't support them
body:
Reproducible with `synth_gatemate` with the following design:
```
module init_sp_ram #(
	parameter ADDR_SIZE = 11,
	parameter DATA_SIZE = 16
) (
	input  clk,
	input [ADDR_SIZE-1:0] addr,
	input [DATA_SIZE-1:0] dat_w,
	input we,
	output reg [DATA_SIZE-1:0] dat_r
);

reg [DATA_SIZE-1:0] mem [0:(1 << ADDR_SIZE)];
initial begin
	$readmemh("ex1.mem", mem);
end

always @(posedge clk) begin
	if(we)
		mem[addr] <= dat_w;
	dat_r <= mem[addr];
end

endmodule
```
and the script:
```
read_verilog init_sp_ram.v
synth_gatemate -top init_sp_ram
```
(File `ex1.mem` needs to exist but contents don't matter.)

This design does not contain any initialized registers, so it should be synthesizable to this architecture. However, when creating emulation circuitry (in this case, for read-first behavior), an initialized `$dff` is generated by `memory_libmap`, which later causes `dfflegalize` to fail.

`memory_libmap` should probably have an option to tell it if the architecture doesn't have initialized registers. For most uses of `ff.val_init` there seems to be an existing else branch X-initializing the flop that we could reuse, but a careful look at the code is required to make sure that still creates correct behavior.
